results.question: How do the sources describe the Roman siege and destruction of Jerusalem?
results.ideal_answer: Josephus, the eyewitness, gives the fullest narrative: Titus encamped about the city, and though he resolved to storm the temple, "that house, God had, for certain, long ago doomed it to the fire" — it was burned as the siege reached its terrible climax of famine and slaughter. Tacitus supplies the Roman view, describing how Titus pitched his camp before the walls and drew up his legions in order of battle against the Jews massed beneath their fortifications. Cassius Dio records the storming of the temple precinct and the city's fall — "thus was Jerusalem destroyed on the very day of Saturn, which even now the Jews reverence most."
results.answer:
The sources describe the Roman siege and destruction of Jerusalem in vivid, often harrowing detail, with each author emphasizing different aspects.

**Josephus**, an eyewitness and the most comprehensive source, provides a blow-by-blow account in *The Wars of the Jews*. He describes the city's formidable triple-wall defenses and the strength of its towers [1], the horrific famine that filled the valleys with corpses and made the stench so unbearable that Titus himself groaned and called God to witness that the slaughter was not his doing [6]. When the temple caught fire, Josephus reports that ten thousand were slain indiscriminately — children, old men, priests, and suppliants alike — and the flames, carried by the wind, made it seem as though the whole city were ablaze [3]. He notes that the temple was burned "against the consent of Caesar" [1], and that Titus, after entering the upper city, marveled at the fortifications and declared that God alone had ejected the Jews from such towers [5]. After the fighting ended, Titus ordered the entire city and temple demolished, sparing only the three tallest towers — Phasaelus, Hippicus, and Mariamne — and a portion of the western wall, both as a garrison camp and as a monument to Roman valor [2]. Josephus also records Titus's speech to the rebel leaders, in which he reproaches them for their madness in bringing their people, city, and holy house to destruction [7].

**Suetonius**, in his life of Vespasian, offers a much briefer summary. He states that Vespasian left the siege of Jerusalem to his son Titus, who displayed great valor and military skill in the enterprise. After an obstinate defense by the Jews, the celebrated city was finally demolished and the glorious temple reduced to ashes — "contrary, however, to the will of Titus, who exerted his utmost efforts to extinguish the flames" [9]. A footnote in Suetonius adds that Jerusalem was taken, sacked, and burnt on the 8th of September, A.D. 69, after a two-year siege [10].

**Tacitus**, in his *Histories*, emphasizes the psychological and supernatural dimensions of the siege. He reports prodigies that preceded the fall: hosts seen battling in the skies, the temple illuminated by a sudden radiance, the doors of the inner shrine flying open, and a voice crying that the gods were departing [12]. He describes the city's internal divisions — three factions under Simon, John, and Eleazar fighting among themselves until the Roman approach forced a reconciliation [14]. Tacitus also details the immense fortifications: walls skillfully bent to expose attackers to flanking fire, towers rising sixty to one hundred and twenty feet, and the temple itself resembling a citadel with its own walls and colonnades [13]. He notes that the total number of the besieged was said to be six hundred thousand, and that men and women alike showed equal resolution, preferring death to abandoning their country [12].

In sum, Josephus provides the most detailed, eyewitness account of the physical destruction and human suffering; Suetonius offers a concise, imperial summary that underscores Titus's reluctance to see the temple burn; and Tacitus frames the event as a cosmic tragedy, highlighting the omens, the internal strife of the Jews, and the sheer scale of the resistance.

results.question: How do the sources portray the reign and death of Herod the Great?
results.ideal_answer: Josephus gives the fullest portrait. In the Antiquities he records the grand works of the reign — in his eighteenth year Herod "undertook a very great work, that is, to build of himself the temple of God" — alongside his implacable cruelty, and then the loathsome final illness in which "his privy-member was putrefied, and produced worms," read as divine punishment. The parallel narrative in the Wars closes the reign with the bare notice that, having survived the killing of his son by only five days, Herod died, "having reigned thirty-four years."
results.answer:
The portrayal of Herod the Great's reign and death in the corpus comes overwhelmingly from Josephus, who provides a detailed and deeply negative account in both his *Antiquities of the Jews* and *The Wars of the Jews*. Strabo's *Geography* mentions Herod only in an editorial footnote, and no other primary source in the corpus (Tacitus, Suetonius, Cassius Dio) contains a narrative of his reign or death.

**Josephus's portrayal of Herod's final illness and death**

Josephus describes Herod's last days as a period of intense physical suffering, mental torment, and calculated cruelty. In his *Antiquities*, he reports that Herod "fell into a distemper" around the age of seventy, grew fierce and bitter, and believed the nation rejoiced in his misfortunes [1]. A sedition led by Judas and Matthias, two popular teachers of the law, further enraged him [1]. Earlier, after the execution of his wife Mariamne, Herod had already suffered a dangerous illness — an inflammation and pain in the back of his head accompanied by madness — which brought him to despair [3].

In his final days, Herod's disease became grotesque. Josephus writes that he was "overborne by his pains," disordered by want of food and a convulsive cough, and attempted suicide by asking for a knife to pare an apple, then lifting his hand as if to stab himself — only to be stopped by his cousin Achiabus [2]. Upon hearing of this, his son Antipater tried to bribe his guards to release him, which prompted Herod to order Antipater's immediate execution [2]. Herod died five days later [2][8][5].

**Herod's cruel final command**

One of the most striking episodes is Herod's plan to ensure universal mourning at his death. Summoning the Jewish nation to the hippodrome at Jericho, he ordered them shut in and then instructed his sister Salome and her husband Alexas to have soldiers slaughter them all as soon as he died, so that "the whole nation would mourn from their very soul" rather than in mockery [4]. This command was never carried out: after Herod's death, Salome released the prisoners, claiming the king had changed his mind [8].

**Testament and succession**

Herod altered his will multiple times in his final days. Initially he bequeathed the kingdom to his youngest son Antipas, out of hatred for Archelaus and Philip [1]. After Antipater's execution, he changed it again, making Archelaus king, Antipas tetrarch of Galilee and Perea, and Philip tetrarch of Gaulonitis, Trachonitis, and Paneas [5][2][8]. He left vast sums to Caesar, Julia, and other Roman notables [1][5].

**Funeral**

Josephus describes a magnificent funeral: a golden bier embroidered with precious stones, a purple bed, the body covered in purple with a diadem and crown of gold and a sceptre in the right hand, escorted by his sons, kindred, guards, and regiments of Thracians, Germans, and Gauls, with five hundred servants carrying spices. The body was carried two hundred furlongs to Herodium for burial [7].

**Josephus's final judgment**

Josephus concludes his account with a damning epitaph: Herod was "a man of great barbarity towards all men equally, and a slave to his passion; but above the consideration of what was right." He acknowledges Herod's extraordinary fortune — rising from a private man to king and surviving countless dangers — but judges him "very unfortunate" in his domestic affairs [5].

**Other sources**

Strabo's *Geography* mentions Herod only in an editorial footnote that summarises his rise to power, his crimes (including the murders of Mariamne, her brother, her mother, and his sons), and the division of his kingdom after his death [6]. No other primary source in the corpus provides a narrative of Herod's reign or death.

results.question: How do the sources describe Vespasian's rise to the throne during the civil war?
results.ideal_answer: The sources agree that Vespasian was raised to the throne by the armies of the East while the Jewish war was still in progress. Josephus, present in his camp, says he "desire[d] to obtain that government, in order to corroborate his attempts upon the whole empire," and was proclaimed by the soldiers in Judaea and Egypt. Tacitus reports the news reaching the East — "messengers from Syria and Judaea brought the news that the provinces of the East had sworn allegiance to him." Suetonius records the acclamation against Vitellius: the troops in Moesia and Pannonia revolted, "as did likewise, of the armies beyond sea, those in Judaea and Syria," who swore allegiance to Vespasian as emperor.
results.answer: The sources describe Vespasian's rise as a combination of military acclamation by the eastern legions and support from key governors. Suetonius reports that the troops in Moesia and Judaea, together with the governor Tiberius Alexander, swore allegiance to Vespasian [1]. Josephus writes that Vespasian coordinated with Tiberius Alexander in Egypt and received the acclaim of the legions and cities; later, in Alexandria, he received news of his confirmation as emperor and sent his son Titus to continue the siege of Jerusalem while he prepared to return to Rome [2][5]. Cassius Dio emphasizes the omens surrounding the civil wars and states that while campaigning against the Jews Vespasian was proclaimed emperor by his soldiers [3]. Tacitus notes that the governor Mucianus and the soldiers of the Syrian army hailed Vespasian as Emperor in Syria [4]. Thus, across the sources the common element is the support of the eastern armies and governors, which set Vespasian on the path to the throne.
results.judge_notes: faithfulness: The answer conflates Suetonius [1] by stating that the Moesian troops swore allegiance alongside Tiberius Alexander and the Judaean army, when [1] records that the Moesian design was quashed and those troops submitted to Vitellius, though the overall narrative of eastern armies and governors supporting Vespasian remains grounded. | completeness: The answer fully addresses how the sources describe Vespasian's rise by detailing the eastern armies' acclamation, support from governors, and the Jewish war context across multiple historians. | attribution: The sources agree on the general narrative of Vespasian's rise to power, and the answer correctly attributes the specific details and perspectives from each source in prose without any misattributions.

results.question: How do the sources narrate the year of the four emperors that followed Nero?
results.ideal_answer: Tacitus gives the connected narrative, opening his Histories "with the time when Servius Galba was consul for the second time with Titus Vinius for his colleague" — the start of the convulsion that ran Galba to Otho to Vitellius to Vespasian. Plutarch's surviving lives carry the same rapid turnover: the senate, swearing in Otho's name the oath it had just sworn and broken to Galba, "conferred on him the titles of Caesar and Augustus." Suetonius' per-emperor lives record each fall in turn — Galba "perished in the seventy-third year of his age, and the seventh month of his reign," cut down in the Forum.
results.answer:
The sources narrate the Year of the Four Emperors as a chaotic and bloody period of civil war following Nero’s suicide. Josephus provides a compressed overview, explaining that he deliberately omits a full account because the events are “well known by all, and they are described by a great number of Greek and Roman authors,” touching on them only briefly for continuity [1]. His narrative highlights Vespasian’s cautious waiting before claiming power and the final battles in Rome, where Vitellius is dragged from his palace, abused, and beheaded, with over fifty thousand slain [6].

Plutarch in his Lives gives detailed, dramatic narratives of Galba and Otho. He describes how Galba, already elderly and weak, was disturbed by the revolts of Vindex and Virginius Rufus, and how the freedman Icelus brought him the news that Nero was dead and the army had declared Galba emperor [9]. Plutarch then narrates Galba’s adoption of Piso as his successor, with Otho’s disappointment and rage at being passed over [2], and the ominous portents that attended the adoption. He continues through Otho’s preparations for war, the indecisive commanders, the Battle of Bedriacum with its tactical details (including the Batavian cavalry attacking the gladiators), and Otho’s noble suicide, which Plutarch notes earned him more praise in death than in life [7][17][12].

Tacitus’s Histories, as preserved, focuses on the moral and political climate. He paints Galba’s entry into Rome as “slow and blood‑stained,” marked by the slaughter of thousands of unarmed soldiers, and describes the weak old emperor dominated by corrupt advisors [11]. The death of Galba is vividly depicted: his standard‑bearer tears down his effigy, he is thrown from his litter near the Lake of Curtius, and his headless trunk is mutilated; Tacitus notes uncertainty about the actual murderer [13]. The crowd’s terror and silence are emphasized [16].

Cassius Dio, in his annalistic fashion, provides a structured account. The table of contents for his Book L (surviving as epitome) lists the key events: the adoption of Piso, Otho’s usurpation, the deaths of Galba and Piso, the battles at Cremona, Otho’s speech and suicide, and the rapacity of Valens [5]. The fuller narrative includes Nero’s last moments and Galba’s assumption of power [10], as well as a detailed and moving account of Otho’s death, where Dio notes that “in life the basest of men he died most nobly” [14].

Suetonius, in his Life of Otho, focuses on the emperor’s character and decisions. He recounts Otho’s rash decision to fight at Bedriacum despite advice to delay, the fraudulent stratagem of the enemy, and his subsequent suicide out of shame for wasting Roman lives [15].

Later scholarship distills the events: William Smith offers a chronological summary from Nero’s death through Galba’s murder, Otho’s suicide, Vitellius’s defeat by Antonius Primus, and Vespasian’s confirmation [3]. Samuel Dill emphasizes the brutal sensibilities of the age, describing how Vitellius gloatingly attended gladiatorial games on the blood‑soaked field of Bedriacum, and how his own end was a grim vengeance for the carnage [18].

Overall, the sources converge on a narrative of rapid succession, military coups, and moral condemnation, but they differ in detail and emphasis: Josephus explicitly abridges, Plutarch and Tacitus supply vivid dramatic scenes, Dio offers a structured chronicle, and Suetonius homes in on individual character.

results.question: How do the sources assess the character and generalship of Hannibal?
results.ideal_answer: The sources agree on his extraordinary gifts while dividing on his vices. Livy's introductory sketch grants him a genius equally fit to command and obey, fearless and tireless, "by far the first of the cavalry and infantry" — but balances it with "inhuman cruelty, more than Punic perfidy." Polybius marvels at the generalship that held a polyglot army together "for sixteen continuous years" in Italy. Appian preserves the famous conversation in which Scipio asked Hannibal whom he thought the greatest general, ranking himself only behind Alexander and Pyrrhus. Mommsen, the modern judge, notes that "the Romans charged him with cruelty, the Carthaginians with covetousness," yet finds the noble image of the commander unmarred.
results.answer:
The ancient sources present a complex and sometimes contradictory assessment of Hannibal's character and generalship. 

**Character:** Livy offers the most detailed portrait, describing Hannibal as possessing extreme courage, endurance, and prudence, but counterbalanced by "inhuman cruelty, more than Punic perfidy, no truth, no reverence for things sacred, no fear of the gods, no respect for oaths, no sense of religion" [1]. Polybius, however, cautions against simplistic judgments, noting that Hannibal's circumstances and the influence of friends like Hannibal Monomachus (who suggested cannibalism) may have led to acts attributed to him, and that his alleged cruelty was partly a product of his situation [4][8]. Polybius also reports that Carthaginians and Massanissa accused Hannibal of avarice, though he himself seems to credit this charge to some extent [8]. Diodorus Siculus highlights Hannibal's cruelty, recounting how he forced Roman captives to fight each other to the death [3]. In contrast, Cassius Dio praises Hannibal's intellectual and physical qualities, describing him as quick in comprehension, skilled in planning, enduring, and well-versed in Phoenician and Greek learning, with a body equally perfected for warfare [2]. 

**Generalship:** The sources unanimously acknowledge Hannibal's exceptional military talent. Livy notes that from his first arrival in Spain, soldiers saw in him the restored image of his father Hamilcar, and that "there never was a genius more fitted for the two most opposite duties of obeying and commanding" [1]. Polybius emphasizes Hannibal's strategic acumen, particularly his ability to understand his opponents' character—as when he correctly judged Flaminius's rashness and exploited it [6]. Polybius also singles out Hannibal's care for his personal safety as a hallmark of his generalship, noting that he never suffered a personal disaster in minor encounters despite many years in enemy territory [9]. Diodorus Siculus calls Hannibal "the most expert general" of the Carthaginians, who kept his diverse army in discipline, never lacked money or provisions, and was loved by mercenaries as much as by his countrymen [5]. Appian preserves a famous conversation in which Hannibal, asked by Scipio to rank the greatest generals, placed Alexander first, Pyrrhus second, and himself third—adding that if he had defeated Scipio, he would have placed himself before Alexander [7]. This self-assessment underscores both his confidence and his recognition of Scipio's superiority. 

Thus, while the sources differ on the moral evaluation of Hannibal's character—some emphasizing cruelty and perfidy, others contextualizing or downplaying these traits—they are virtually unanimous in celebrating his brilliance as a commander.

results.question: How do the sources describe the final destruction of Carthage?
results.ideal_answer: Appian gives the fullest narrative of 146 BC: as the city of 700 years came to its end, Scipio "is said to have shed tears and publicly lamented the fortune of the enemy," reflecting on the mutability that would one day overtake Rome itself. Polybius, his tutor and an eyewitness, records the same reflection on the fall of "the once mighty empires of the Assyrians, Medes, Persians" and Macedon, with Scipio quoting Homer on the doom of Troy. The epitome of Livy registers the bare outcome — Scipio "at length took the city by storm, in the seven hundredth year after its foundation."
results.answer:
The sources describe the final destruction of Carthage in 146 BCE as a multi-stage horror: a brutal six-day street-by-street sack, a catastrophic fire, and a deliberate ritual erasure of the city ordered by the Roman Senate.

**The sack and the fire.** Appian provides the most graphic account of the destruction as it unfolded. As fire spread through the city, soldiers did not demolish buildings piecemeal but pulled them down in heaps, so that "the crashing grew louder, and many corpses fell with the stones into the midst" [1]. The living — especially the old, women, and children hidden in the innermost rooms — were thrust out and fell from heights with stones, timbers, and fire, "torn asunder in all shapes of horror, crushed and mangled" [1]. Street-cleaners clearing the rubble with axes and forks tossed the dead and the still-living together into trenches, where some writhed with their legs sticking out of the ground while horses trampled their faces [1]. William Smith notes that the fighting and butchery lasted six days [5].

**The human tragedy on the citadel.** After the main population surrendered — Mommsen gives the figure of 30,000 men and 25,000 women, "not the tenth part of the former population" [2] — the Roman deserters (900 in number) and the Carthaginian general Hasdrubal, with his wife and two children, took refuge in the temple of the God of Healing [2]. Polybius, an eyewitness, records that Hasdrubal secretly abandoned his post and threw himself as a suppliant at Scipio's knees, prompting Scipio to remark on the mutability of fortune [7]. When Hasdrubal's wife saw him grovelling, "her proud heart swelled at this disgrace brought on her dear perishing home, and, with bitter words bidding her husband be careful to save his life, she plunged first her sons and then herself into the flames" [2]. Cassius Dio gives the same account of the wife casting her children and herself into the fire [8].

**Scipio's reaction.** Appian reports that Scipio, beholding the city that had flourished for 700 years now come to "total destruction," shed tears and reflected aloud on the rise and fall of empires, naming Troy, Assyria, Media, Persia, and Macedon. When Polybius asked what he meant, Scipio replied that he feared the same fate for Rome [6]. Smith adds that Scipio is said to have quoted Homer's *Iliad* over the flames: "The day shall come when sacred Troy shall perish, and Priam and his people shall be slain" [5].

**The final erasure.** After the sack, much of the city still stood, and Scipio may have wished to preserve it, but the Senate ordered otherwise [3]. The Senate commanded that Carthage and its suburb Magalia be levelled to the ground, that a plough be passed over the site to legally extinguish the city's existence, and that the soil be cursed forever so that neither house nor cornfield might reappear [3][4]. The ruins burned for seventeen days; Mommsen notes that modern excavations of the city wall uncovered a layer of ashes four to five feet deep, filled with half-charred wood, iron fragments, and projectiles [3]. Cassius Dio records that the city was "overthrown from pinnacle to foundation" and that it was decreed accursed for anyone to settle on the site [8].

results.question: How do the sources recount Rome's long wars against Mithridates of Pontus?

Plutarch follows the commanders: the people "unanimously voted [Lucullus] to the Mithridatic war," which Pompey later took over. Livy's epitome records the war's terrible opening — Mithridates seized Asia, chained the proconsul and the general, "and ordered all the Romans in Asia to be massacred on the same day."

results.question: How do the sources treat the reforms and deaths of the Gracchi brothers?
results.ideal_answer: Plutarch preserves the moral force of the cause in Tiberius Gracchus's speech on the landless soldiers of Italy, who though they fought for Rome had no "houses of their own, or hearths of their ancestors to defend." Appian narrates the legislative struggle, ending when a substitute tribune was chosen "and the agrarian law was enacted." Livy's epitome summarizes the reform itself — Gracchus's agrarian law that "no person should hold more than five hundred acres of the public lands" — and the violence its passage unleashed.
results.answer:
The sources treat the reforms and deaths of the Gracchi brothers with notable differences in tone, detail, and judgment.

**Plutarch** provides the most vivid and sympathetic biographical account. He traces Tiberius's motivation to his journey through Tuscany, where he found the countryside depopulated and worked by imported slaves rather than free farmers [17]. Plutarch includes Tiberius's famous rhetorical appeal — that "the wild beasts of Italy have their dens" while Roman soldiers "wander from place to place with their wives and children" [19] — and emphasizes that the agrarian law was drawn up with the advice of eminent citizens like Crassus the high-priest and Mucius Scaevola [17]. He treats the deposition of Octavius as a desperate but dangerous constitutional violation [21], and describes Tiberius's death as a conspiracy driven by the hatred of the rich, noting the cruelty shown to his corpse — denied burial by his own brother and thrown into the river [16]. For Gaius, Plutarch details a broad reform program: land distribution, clothing soldiers at public expense, extending voting rights to Italians, cheap corn, and judicial reform adding equestrians to senatorial juries [18]. He notes that Gaius avoided armed resistance even when his life was threatened [20].

**Appian's Civil Wars** treats the Gracchi as the opening episode of the Roman revolution. He highlights Tiberius's stated motive as concern for Italy's manpower — "not wealth, but an increase of efficient population" [7] — and presents the conflict in structural terms as a class struggle between rich possessors and the landless poor. His account of Tiberius's death is dramatic: the Senate marched to the Capitol led by Nasica, who wrapped his toga around his head and cried "Let those who would save the country follow me"; Gracchus was slain near the temple by the statues of the kings, and the bodies were thrown into the Tiber [24]. Appian calls the affair "shocking" and the first such violence in Roman politics, adding that Tiberius pursued a "most excellent design" but "in too violent a manner" [24]. For Gaius, Appian records that his head and Flaccus's were brought to the consul Opimius, who paid their weight in gold, and that the Senate ordered a temple of Concord built after the bloodshed [12]. He also notes that Gaius's land law was later undermined, allowing the rich to buy up the poor's allotments [12].

**Livy's Epitome** offers a terse, unsympathetic summary. It states that Tiberius "wrought himself up to such a degree of passion" that he deposed his colleague Octavius, appointed himself and his family as land commissioners, and proposed using Attalus's bequest to fund distributions [1]. The epitome frames the Senate's indignation as a response to "repeated ill-treatment" and records Tiberius's death matter-of-factly: he was slain in the Capitol by the chief nobles on Nasica's advice, beaten with fragments of seats, and thrown into the river without burial [1].

**Mommsen (scholarship)** provides legal and political analysis. He treats the agrarian law as a renewal of the Licinio-Sextian law of 387 and argues that the distribution of domains was legally sound but politically explosive because it asserted dormant state claims against long-established possessors [11][25]. He is sharply critical of Tiberius's methods — the deposition of Octavius was "revolution against the spirit of the constitution" — but acknowledges that Tiberius's aim of checking the decline of the Italian farmer-class was approved by men like Scipio Aemilianus and Gaius Laelius [25]. Mommsen presents Tiberius's escalation into further reforms (Attalus treasure, shortening service, Italian citizenship) as driven by the logic of self-preservation once he had alienated the aristocracy [8]. He treats the assassination of Scipio Aemilianus, who opposed the commission, as a political murder instigated by the Gracchan party [14].

In sum: Plutarch is biographical and sympathetic, focusing on character and dramatic pathos; Appian is a political historian emphasizing the Gracchi as the origin of the civil wars; Livy's epitome is a laconic, establishment-friendly summary; and Mommsen offers analytical distance, approving the agrarian goal while condemning the revolutionary means. All agree on the basic facts — the 500-jugera limit, the deposition of Octavius, the use of Attalus's bequest, and the violent deaths — but differ markedly in their judgments and narrative emphasis.

results.question: How do the sources describe the war against Jugurtha of Numidia?
results.ideal_answer: The sources frame the war as much by Roman corruption as by arms. Appian preserves Jugurtha's famous verdict on the venal city — that "the whole city of Rome could be bought if a purchaser could be found for it." Plutarch follows the endgame through the Numidian kings: after Jugurtha fled and "in his distress came to [Bocchus] as his last hope," he was received as a suppliant and then betrayed. Livy's epitome records the capture plainly — Bocchus, beaten and unwilling to fight on, "delivered up Jugurtha in chains to Marius."
results.answer: The sources describe the Jugurthine War (111–106 BC) as a conflict marked by Roman corruption, military setbacks, and eventual success through the efforts of Metellus, Marius, and Sulla. William Smith's *A Smaller History of Rome* provides the most detailed narrative account [3][5][4][6][8]. It reports that the war arose after Jugurtha assassinated his cousin Hiempsal, defeated and eventually killed his other cousin Adherbal at Cirta, and repeatedly frustrated Rome through bribery. When Rome finally declared war in 111 BC, the consul Calpurnius Bestia was bribed into granting a favorable peace, sparking outrage at Rome [4][10]. Jugurtha was summoned to Rome under safe-conduct but continued his intrigues there, murdering a rival claimant named Massiva; upon departing, he allegedly declared Rome "a city for sale, and destined to perish quickly, if it can find a purchaser" [6][10]. Military incompetence followed under Spurius Postumius Albinus and his brother Aulus, whose army was defeated and forced to pass under the yoke [6]. A turning point came with the consul Q. Caecilius Metellus (109 BC), who restored discipline and defeated Jugurtha in multiple battles [6][10]. However, Jugurtha escaped and allied with his father-in-law Bocchus, king of Mauretania, prolonging the war [2][8]. Marius, Metellus's ambitious lieutenant, returned to Rome and secured the consulship and command of the Numidian War through popular support [2][9][8]. Marius gradually reduced Jugurtha's strongholds, but Mommsen notes that "the conquest of Numidia up to the edge of the desert was the work of Metellus, the capture of Jugurtha was the work of Sulla, and between the two Marius played a part somewhat compromising the dignity of an ambitious upstart" [9]. The war ended in 106 BC when Sulla, Marius's quaestor, persuaded Bocchus to betray Jugurtha, who was seized during a pretended conference and delivered in chains to the Romans [9][8][7]. Jugurtha was later paraded in Marius's triumph and executed in the Tullianum prison [9].

results.question: How do the sources tell the rise and fall of Theban supremacy under Epaminondas?
results.ideal_answer: The sources trace Theban hegemony as the personal achievement of Epaminondas — and its collapse with his death. Xenophon, contemporary and cool toward Thebes, ends his history on the inconclusive note that after Mantinea "the effective result of these achievements was the very opposite of that which the world at large anticipated." Diodorus makes the dependence explicit: while Epaminondas lived "he gained the sovereign power for his country; but, by his death they lost it again" — "and this was the end of Epaminondas, a man honoured and esteemed of all." Pausanias records his monument, whose elegiac lines tell "how he restored Messene, and how the Greeks got their freedom through him." Plutarch supplies the Theban view of the bond with Pelopidas, for whom Epaminondas "singly fought a multitude, resolving rather to die than forsake his helpless" friend.
results.answer:
The sources tell the rise and fall of Theban supremacy through a combination of contemporary disdain, belated admiration, and retrospective judgment. The key phases—the victory at Leuctra, the brief Theban predominance, and the fatal campaign of Mantinea—are handed down by Xenophon, Diodorus Siculus, and Plutarch, with modern historians such as Grote and Bury interpreting their biases.

**The Rise: Leuctra and after**
The foundation of Theban power is the battle of Leuctra (371 BCE). Diodorus Siculus gives a straightforward account: Epaminondas and his Boeotians, “engaging with the most famous warriors of Greece, and … victorious with an army far short in number,” killed four thousand Lacedaemonians for only three hundred Boeotians [3]. Xenophon, who detests Thebes’s success, is notoriously stingy with credit. Grote notes that in Xenophon’s *Hellenica* the name of Epaminondas is entirely omitted from the Leuctra narrative, though Xenophon “recognizes in substance that the battle was decided by the irresistible Theban force brought to bear upon one point of the enemy’s phalanx”—the novel deep column that “betokened high military genius” [8][4]. The victory shattered Sparta’s military prestige, and Thebes immediately followed it up: Epaminondas invaded Laconia, ravaged its virgin territory, and “cut off the best half of it for the restitution of independent Messênê, and erected the hostile Arcadian community of Megalopolis” [2]. Xenophon concedes some of these campaigns, such as Epaminondas’s march into Achaea, where he left the aristocrats in power on his personal authority, though the Theban home government undid his work by imposing democracies [16]. Bury sums up the transformation: after Leuctra “the parts hitherto played by the two states are reversed. Thebes now becomes the invader of the Peloponnese, as Sparta before had been the invader of Boeotia” [6].

**The Fall: Mantinea and Epaminondas’s death**
The zenith was brief. In 362 BCE Epaminondas led a final expedition into the Peloponnesus. Xenophon, for once, gives him a detailed, almost panegyrical description of his last campaign. The general made a dash toward an undefended Sparta, which would have fallen “like a nest of young birds” had a Cretan not warned Agesilaus in time [15]. Repulsed from Sparta, he concentrated on Mantinea. There he repeated the Leuctrian tactic, massing his best troops on the left and holding the rest back; “the irresistible charge, both of infantry and cavalry,” broke the enemy’s line [7][9]. As he pursued the fleeing Lacedaemonians, “he received a mortal thrust from a spear” [17][9]. Xenophon’s narrative ends with a remarkable encomium: he praises Epaminondas’s “comprehensive foresight,” “excellent discipline,” and “fertility of aggressive resource,” acknowledging that only accident prevented him from becoming “arbiter of Peloponnesus” [5]. Plutarch’s *Agesilaus* adds that the Spartans so dreaded Epaminondas that they “decreed honors and gifts” to his slayer and exempted his posterity from taxes [17].

**Aftermath and judgment**
All sources agree that Theban supremacy died with Epaminondas. Grote remarks that “with him the dignity and commanding influence of Thebes both began and ended” [2]. Xenophon, despite his pro-Spartan bias, confirms that Thebes gained no new territory or lasting dominion from the battle [1]. The sources thus portray a meteoric rise, driven by one man’s genius, and an abrupt fall on the battlefield where he fell.

results.question: How do the sources describe the Spartan system of government attributed to Lycurgus?
results.ideal_answer: The sources credit Lycurgus with a mixed, balanced constitution. Plutarch singles out the central institution: "the first and of greatest importance was the establishment of the senate," a council of twenty-eight elders whose power, equal to the kings', acted "like ballast in a ship" between monarchy and democracy. Herodotus gives the early Greek account of the same transformation — once Lycurgus had reformed their institutions "the Spartans had good laws," and after his death "they erected a temple" to him and paid him great worship.
results.answer:
The sources describe the Spartan system of government attributed to Lycurgus as a mixed constitution combining elements of monarchy, oligarchy, and democracy. Herodotus, the earliest source, states that Lycurgus, a respected Spartan who visited Delphi, changed all the laws and established military divisions (Enomoties and Triecads), the common meals (syssitia), the ephors, and the senate [3]. He mentions a variant story that Lycurgus brought these institutions from Crete while acting as guardian for his nephew, the king, and that the Spartans built him a temple and worship him [3].

Plutarch provides a much fuller account. He reports that Lycurgus' first and most important reform was the establishment of the senate, consisting of twenty-eight elders who, together with the two kings, made thirty. This senate served as a stabilizing force: the twenty-eight would side with the kings to resist democracy, yet support the people against absolute monarchy [4]. Plutarch also gives the text of the 'Rhetra,' the Delphic oracle that authorized the constitution. The Rhetra commanded the creation of a council of thirty elders (including the kings) and periodic assemblies of the people between Babyca and Cnacion, where 'the commons have the final voice and decision' [5]. In practice, however, the assembly was not allowed to debate; it could only ratify or reject what the kings or senate proposed [6].

Plutarch further notes that later, because the people sometimes distorted proposals by adding or omitting words, kings Polydorus and Theopompus inserted a clause providing that if the people decided 'crookedly,' the elders and leaders could dismiss the assembly [6]. Plutarch places the creation of the ephors much later—about 130 years after Lycurgus—as a 'bit' to check the still-dominant oligarchical element, and records the reply of king Theopompus to his wife that this reduction of royal power actually made the kingship 'greater, for it will last longer' [6]. Herodotus, however, ascribes the ephors directly to Lycurgus [3], a disagreement noted by later scholarship.

In sum, the ancient sources present Lycurgus as the architect of a balanced constitution with two hereditary kings, a council of elders (the Gerousia), a popular assembly with limited powers, and an executive board of five ephors—the last either original or a later addition—which together made Sparta’s government a mixture of kingly, aristocratic, and popular elements [3][4][5][6]. Later commentary, such as that of Bury, emphasizes this mixed character and notes the constitution’s long stability [7].

results.question: What do the sources report about the oracle at Delphi — its setting, procedure, and authority?
results.ideal_answer: The sources present Delphi as the most authoritative and richest of Greek shrines. Strabo records its divine charter — that "Apollo, by the aid of Themis, established this oracle with a view to benefit the human race." Pausanias preserves its ancient history, that "in the most ancient times it was the oracle of Earth" before it passed to Apollo. Diodorus shows the other side of its fame, the immense treasure that made it a target: in the Sacred War the Phocian leader's "brother and successor Onomarchus converted much of those treasures" to pay his troops, the sacrilege that defined the conflict.

results.question: How do the sources explain the fall of Nineveh and the end of the Assyrian empire?
results.ideal_answer: The modern reconstruction and the ancient notice agree that Nineveh fell to a coalition led by the Medes. Rawlinson's Assyria judges that the empire "fell, not so much from any inherent weakness, or from the effect of gradual decay, as by an unfortunate combination of circumstances" — a barbarian inroad coinciding with the rise of a warlike neighbour. His Media traces that neighbour's role: after the siege "the fall of the city was followed by a division of the spoil between the two principal conquerors," Cyaxares the Mede taking Assyria proper. Herodotus gives the early Greek account — the Medes "had rule over the same nations as before; and they also took Nineveh" and made the Assyrians subject, except Babylon.
results.answer:
The sources in the corpus explain the fall of Nineveh and the end of the Assyrian empire through a combination of military, natural, and internal factors, though they also note disagreements among the ancient authorities.

**The military alliance and betrayal.** The final blow came from a combined army of Medes under Cyaxares and Babylonians under Nabopolassar (or his son Nebuchadnezzar) [4][7]. According to Rawlinson, the Assyrian king Saracus had sent his general Nabopolassar to defend Babylon against an enemy advancing from the sea, but Nabopolassar betrayed his sovereign: he secretly allied with Cyaxares, married his son Nebuchadnezzar to the Median king's daughter, and then joined forces with the Medes to attack Nineveh itself [6]. Grote notes that this version — Medes and Babylonians acting together — comes from Ctesias, Berosus, and Josephus, whereas Herodotus presents the Medes alone under Cyaxares as the conquerors of Nineveh, without mentioning the Babylonians [1].

**The Tigris flood.** A dramatic element in Ctesias's account, preserved by Rawlinson, is that after a siege lasting over two years, unusually heavy rains swelled the Tigris River so greatly that it burst into the city and swept away more than two miles of the wall [4]. Rawlinson connects this to the biblical prophet Nahum, whose oracle declares that "the gates of the rivers are opened, and the palace is dissolved" — suggesting that a sluice or floodgate at the northwest angle of Nineveh gave way under the pressure, allowing a torrent of water to undermine the northern wall and rush into the city [3].

**The king's suicide.** When the breach made further resistance impossible, the last Assyrian monarch — called Sardanapalus by Ctesias, Saracus by Berosus — burned himself alive in his palace along with his concubines, eunuchs, and chief wealth [4][6]. Rawlinson accepts this self-immolation as a reliable fact, since it is confirmed by Abydenus, who almost certainly follows Berosus [7].

**Prior weakening by the Scythians.** Rawlinson also attributes the empire's rapid decline to the devastating Scythian invasion that preceded the final siege. The Scythians ravaged and depopulated the country, plundered the provinces, sacked many great towns, burned the old kings' palaces, and carried off the gold and silver. After their departure, Assyria was "but the shadow of her former self" — weak, exhausted, and vulnerable to a permanent conqueror [5].

**Divine prophecy.** Finally, Josephus records that the prophet Nahum foretold the overthrow of Nineveh in vivid terms — describing it becoming "a pool of water in motion," its people fleeing in panic and contention, and the lion (symbol of Assyrian power) no longer going out to "give laws to the world" — a prophecy he says was fulfilled 115 years later [2].

results.question: What do the sources say about the walls, size, and wonders of Babylon?
results.ideal_answer: The sources concur on Babylon's vast scale. Rawlinson's modern survey calls it "probably the largest and most magnificent city of the ancient world." Herodotus describes its plan — "there are two divisions of the city; for a river whose name is Euphrates parts it in the middle" — within its enormous square of wall. Strabo gives the measurements: the wall was 385 "stadia in circumference, and 32 feet in thickness," with towers rising to 60 cubits — the defenses that, with the hanging garden, made the city one of the wonders of the world.
results.answer:
The sources in the corpus offer several accounts of Babylon's walls, size, and wonders, with Herodotus and Strabo providing the most detailed ancient descriptions, while later historians Rawlinson and Grote compare and critique these figures.

**Herodotus** describes Babylon as lying in a great plain, square in shape, with each side measuring 120 furlongs (480 stades circuit) [1]. He states the walls were made of baked brick and asphalt, with reed mats inserted every thirty courses. The walls were 50 royal cubits thick and 200 royal cubits high (the royal cubit being three fingers longer than the common cubit), with 100 bronze gates. A deep, broad, water-filled trench encircled the walls, and at the top, chambers faced each other with space for a four-horse chariot to turn. He calls it more magnificent than all other known cities [1].

**Strabo** reports the wall's circuit as 385 stades, with a thickness of 32 feet; the height between towers was 50 cubits, the towers themselves 60 cubits. The roadway on the wall allowed four-horse chariots to pass each other. He explicitly counts both the wall and the Hanging Garden among the seven wonders of the world. He describes the Hanging Garden as a square structure (four plethra per side) of vaulted terraces on cube-shaped pillars, filled with earth to support large trees, watered by engines raising water from the Euphrates. He also mentions the tomb of Belus, a quadrangular pyramid of baked brick a stadium high and wide, then in ruins [5].

**Modern commentators** note the wide discrepancies among ancient sources. Rawlinson sums up the circuit estimates: Herodotus gives 480 stades (about 56 miles); Ctesias 360 stades (41 miles); Alexander's historians 365, 368, or 385 stades, with a consensus around 40 miles [9]. On wall height, Rawlinson records Herodotus' 200 royal cubits (about 335 English feet), Ctesias' 50 fathoms (300 ordinary Greek feet), Pliny's 235 feet, and later writers' figures down to 75 feet. He concludes the numbers are either rough guesses or exaggerations, but the walls were undeniably very high and thick [2]. Grote similarly compares the figures and defends Herodotus' credibility, noting that Ctesias' 50 fathoms equal 200 common cubits, only a little less than Herodotus' figure [4][8]. Thus the sources paint Babylon as a city of astonishing size and grandeur, though they disagree on exact measurements.

results.question: How do the sources describe Rome's wars against the Sassanid Persian empire?
results.ideal_answer: The sources mark the Sassanid dynasty as a renewed and formidable threat to Rome on the eastern frontier. Cassius Dio records its alarming birth — "Artaxerxes, a Persian, having conquered the Parthians in three battles and killed their king," then menaced the Roman provinces of Mesopotamia. Rawlinson's Sassanian volume traces the long struggle over the frontier fortresses, noting how Sapor "rightly conceived from the first the importance of Nisibis." Ammianus, the eyewitness of Julian's invasion, shows the Persians' method against the retreating Romans — having "learnt by their frequent defeats to shun pitched battles," they "laid secret ambuscades on our road."
results.answer:
The Roman-Sassanid wars are described in the corpus through three main lenses: the comprehensive Victorian scholarship of George Rawlinson, the critical Enlightenment history of Edward Gibbon, and the vivid eyewitness account of the 4th-century soldier Ammianus Marcellinus.

Rawlinson’s "Seven Great Monarchies, Vol. 7 — The Sassanian Empire" provides a detailed, continuous narrative of the entire series of conflicts, from the initial challenges of Artaxerxes (Ardashir I) against Alexander Severus [23][24] through the wars of Sapor I (Shapur I), including the capture of Valerian [25], and culminating in the great struggle of Sapor II (Shapur II) with Constantius and Julian [3][15][2][4][7]. His account is analytical, frequently evaluating the leadership and military qualities of both sides. For example, he judges Sapor II as a general who "was never once defeated" and proved superior to Constantius and Jovian, and "not unequal to Julian" [9]. He details the repeated sieges of Nisibis, the battles before Ctesiphon, and the eventual Persian recovery of Mesopotamia, always with an eye to the broader strategic picture and the character of the rulers.

Gibbon’s "Decline and Fall" covers many of the same events but with a critical, sometimes ironic, tone. He draws on a wide range of ancient authorities (cited in his footnotes) and often highlights the futility and horror of the wars. In recounting Julian’s expedition, Gibbon notes the surprise of Sapor, who "had been so long accustomed to the tardy ostentation of Constantius," and describes the Persian king’s grief and anxiety upon learning of the devastation of Assyria [5]. He also presents the council that dissuaded Julian from besieging Ctesiphon, remarking that "it is not easy for us to conceive" how a city thrice taken before could now be impregnable [14], thus implicitly questioning the strategic decision. For Constantius’ earlier wars, he bluntly states that the emperor suffered many heavy losses and "no prosperous battle" against Sapor except at Singara, while noting that later panegyrics by Julian and Libanius were recanted after Constantius’ death [10].

Ammianus Marcellinus, a participant in Julian’s 363 campaign, supplies the most immediate and dramatic descriptions. His "Roman History" recounts the crossing of the Tigris, the hard-fought battle before Ctesiphon where the Romans drove the Persians back to the walls but were checked by the wounded Victor [19], the capture and burning of a castle near the city [13], and the fateful decision to abandon the siege and burn the fleet, which Ammianus calls "rashly" done [16]. He also records the grim aftermath: after Julian’s death, the army, worn down by hunger and constant attacks, was forced into a "necessary but disgraceful peace" with Sapor, surrendering Nisibis, Singara, and five provinces [20]. Ammianus thus provides a soldier’s-eye view of the hardships of desert warfare, the chaos of battle, and the bitterness of defeat.

Across all three sources, the wars are depicted as prolonged, strategically focused on the fortified cities of Mesopotamia (Nisibis, Singara, Amida, Ctesiphon), marked by alternating Roman invasions and Persian counterattacks, and colored by the larger-than-life personalities of rulers such as Sapor II and Julian. Rawlinson offers the fullest chronological sweep and moral verdicts; Gibbon contributes a detached, often skeptical, commentary; and Ammianus supplies the vivid, on-the-ground details and a participant’s judgment of the events he witnessed.

results.question: How do the sources describe Rome's catastrophic defeat by the Parthians at Carrhae?
results.ideal_answer: Plutarch's life of Crassus gives the fullest narrative of the disaster — the Romans, after the battle, had "a sad night of it," unable to bury their dead or tend their wounded, "everyone bewailed his own fate." Cassius Dio supplies the end at the parley: lured to a conference by Surena, Crassus "fell among the rest, whether he was slain by one of his own men to prevent his capture alive, or whether by the enemy." Rawlinson's Parthia explains the military cause from the victors' side — to attack a cavalry power "with a body of foot-soldiers, supported by an insignificant number of horse, must be at all times rash and dangerous."
results.answer:
The sources describe Rome's defeat at Carrhae (53 BC) as one of the most catastrophic disasters in Roman history, with several key elements:

**The Battle and Tactical Mismatch**

Plutarch recounts that the Romans were led into the open plains of Mesopotamia by a treacherous Arabian guide, Andromachus, who was in the pay of the Parthian general Surena [4]. The Parthians used their cavalry and archers to devastating effect: they "worried the densely-marshaled Romans with showers of arrows" and used feigned retreats to draw them into disadvantageous positions [7]. Mommsen emphasizes that this was a military revolution — "the Roman weapons of close combat and the Roman system of concentration yielded for the first time before the weapons of more distant warfare" [3]. Cassius Dio describes the Romans' helplessness in detail: if they locked shields to avoid arrows, the Parthian lancers charged them; if they spread out to avoid the lancers, they were shot with arrows. "Neither course afforded them safety, and both were fraught with destruction" [8].

**The Death of Publius Crassus**

The younger Crassus, who had served under Caesar in Gaul, led a cavalry charge that was lured away from the main body and surrounded. Plutarch says he "threw himself in desperation with his Celtic cavalry" against the iron-clad Parthian lancers, but his men were cut down; he refused to abandon his troops and was killed [2]. Mommsen adds that he "caused himself to be stabbed by the hand of his shield-bearer" [6]. Cassius Dio reports that his head was displayed before his father's eyes on a pole [8].

**The Retreat and Treachery**

The Romans retreated to Carrhae (Haran) under cover of night, but the wounded were left behind with "strange confusion and disorder, with an outcry and lamentation" [2]. Crassus was then deceived again by Andromachus, who led him into marshes and ditches rather than to safety [1]. Surena, the Parthian general, offered a truce and a personal conference. Plutarch describes how the Romans, "compassing Crassus about, and fencing him with their shields, declared proudly, that no arrow in Parthia should ever touch their general" [1].

**The Death of Crassus**

At the conference, the Parthians attempted to seize Crassus. In the ensuing scuffle, he was killed. Plutarch says he "fell among the rest" [1]; Cassius Dio notes uncertainty: "whether he was slain by one of his own men to prevent his capture alive, or whether by the enemy because he was wounded anyway" [10]. William Smith simply states he "was treacherously seized, and, in the scuffle which ensued, was slain by some unknown hand" [7].

**The Mockery with Gold**

Multiple sources report that the Parthians poured molten gold into Crassus's mouth in mockery of his notorious greed. Cassius Dio says "the Parthians, as some say, poured gold into his mouth" [10]. William Smith records that the Parthian king Orodes "caused melted gold to be poured into the mouth, saying, 'Sate thyself now with that metal of which in life thou wert so greedy'" [7]. Mommsen also notes this detail [9].

**The Scale of the Disaster**

Mommsen provides the starkest figures: of over 40,000 Roman legionaries who crossed the Euphrates, "not a fourth part returned; the half had perished; nearly 10,000 Roman prisoners were settled by the victors in the extreme east of their kingdom" [9]. William Smith gives similar numbers: "Twenty thousand Roman troops were slain, and ten thousand taken prisoners" [7]. Mommsen ranks the disaster alongside "the days of the Allia, of Cannae, and of Arausio" [9]. Only a small portion under Cassius Longinus escaped to Syria [7][9].

results.question: What do the sources reveal about Roman dining, banquets, and table luxury?
results.ideal_answer: The sources show both the ordinary custom and its imperial excess. Fowler describes the shape of the Roman day around its chief meal — "the dinner, cena, was in fact the principal private event of the day," coming when all business was over. Suetonius supplies the extreme of imperial gluttony, the emperor Vitellius who "always made three meals a day, sometimes four: breakfast, dinner, and supper, and a drunken revel after all," sustained by habitual vomiting. Dill, the modern voice, weighs the moralists' charges, noting that "modern criticism has laboured hard to correct some of the harsher judgments on the luxury and self-indulgence of the period of the early Empire."
results.answer: The sources reveal a broad arc of Roman dining, from old‑fashioned simplicity to extravagant luxury, and they do so through both moralizing narratives and vivid description. According to W. Warde Fowler, in early times meals were taken in the atrium with the whole household, the husband reclining on a couch while wife and children sat; dinner was merely a break in work [12]. After the Punic wars, however, dinner‑parties became fashionable, later hours were adopted, and the traditional three‑course structure (gustatio, cena proper, dessert) could be stretched almost without limit by Greek‑trained slave‑cooks [1]. Theodor Mommsen pinpoints the influx of Asiatic luxury after the expedition to Asia Minor in 564 AUC (190 BC), noting that before that time Romans had only one hot meal a day; afterwards, hot dishes appeared even at the midday prandium, scientific cookery emerged, and special cooks were kept in wealthy houses [6]. The table became the very centre of upper‑class life: villas were built with separate winter and summer dining‑rooms, dinner was sometimes served in picture‑galleries or aviaries, and the master might even instruct his cooks [15]. Marine fish and oysters displaced plain roasts, Italian river‑fish were banished from good tables, and imported Greek wines like Chian and Lesbian were served even at popular festivals [8]. The demand for exotic dainties drove prices to astonishing heights—up to 100,000 sesterces for a single cook—and sumptuary laws repeatedly tried to cap expenses, but almost no one of rank obeyed them [4][14]. Mommsen also records the menu of a pontifical banquet attended by Julius Caesar and the Vestals, which began with sea‑urchins, oysters, and fattened fowls, and offered sow’s udder, boar’s head, ducks, hares, and elaborate pastries, confirming Varro’s observation that such college‑banquets inflated the cost of all delicacies [3]. Samuel Dill adds that the luxury of the table reached its zenith between Actium and Vespasian, when enormous fortunes were spent on citrus‑wood tables, ivory couches, gold‑plated ceilings, and hundreds of household slaves [18]. He also relates Petronius’ satirical portrait of the freedman Trimalchio, whose banquet featured a boar from which live thrushes flew, a dish representing the twelve signs of the Zodiac, and a descending ceiling‑tray with Priapus bearing fruits and sweets, while hundred‑year‑old Falernian was served [5][2]. Cassius Dio provides Nero’s aquatic feast: couches resting on floats in a flooded theatre, with brothels lining the banks and noblewomen compelled to serve the revellers [16]. On a slightly more restrained level, Suetonius notes that Julius Caesar entertained constantly, kept his table “plentiful and elegant,” but limited the number of courses to three or six and was noted for his courteous conversation rather than lavish fare [20]. Plutarch’s Life of Lucullus offers the opposite extreme: when Cicero and Pompey asked to dine with him without advance warning, the resourceful Lucullus still produced a magnificent meal, and when his steward once served a modest dinner because no guests were expected, Lucullus replied, “What, did you not know that to‑day Lucullus dines with Lucullus?” [19]. Finally, Ammianus Marcellinus, writing in the fourth century, complains of long and unwholesome banquets at which invitations were sold by nomenclators and learned men were shunned in favour of charioteer‑fanciers and dice‑players [7]. Across all these sources, one sees the same pattern: moralists lamenting a luxury that seemed to have no bounds, yet society continuing to find ever more ingenious ways to celebrate its wealth at the dinner table.

results.question: How many wounds did Julius Caesar receive when he was assassinated?
results.ideal_answer: According to Suetonius, Caesar was stabbed with twenty-three wounds. He uttered a groan at the first wound but no cry, though some report that he said "And you too, my son?" to Marcus Brutus.

results.question: What period of history did Gibbon declare the happiest and most prosperous for the human race?
results.ideal_answer: Gibbon wrote that if asked to fix the period during which the condition of the human race was most happy and prosperous, one would without hesitation name the span from the death of Domitian to the accession of Commodus — the era of Nerva, Trajan, Hadrian, and the Antonines.

results.question: According to Tacitus, what did the Germans consider the greatest disgrace in battle?
results.ideal_answer: Tacitus says the greatest disgrace that could befall a German warrior was to have abandoned his shield. He adds that for a chief's companions it was lifelong reproach and infamy to retreat from the field surviving their chief.
results.answer: According to Tacitus, the greatest disgrace that could befall a German in battle was to have abandoned his shield. Those branded with this ignominy were barred from religious rites and public assemblies, and many who survived battle resorted to hanging themselves to escape the infamy [1].

results.question: Whom did the Athenians send as a runner to Sparta before the battle of Marathon, and what happened to him on the way?
results.ideal_answer: The Athenian generals sent Pheidippides, a professional long-distance runner, as herald to Sparta. On the way, by Mount Parthenion above Tegea, the god Pan met him, called him by name, and bade him ask the Athenians why they paid him no honor although he was well disposed to them and had often been — and would again be — serviceable.
results.answer: The Athenians sent Pheidippides, an Athenian professional long-distance runner, as a herald to Sparta before the battle of Marathon [1]. On his way, as he himself reported, the god Pan met him by Mount Parthenion (above Tegea). Pan called out his name and told him to ask the Athenians why they had no care for him, despite being well-disposed and helpful to them on many occasions, and promising future aid [1]. The Athenians believed this account and later established a temple of Pan under the Acropolis, with annual sacrifices and a torch-race in his honor [1].

results.question: What epitaph was inscribed for the Spartans who fell at Thermopylae?
results.ideal_answer: The inscription for the Spartans read (in Macaulay's translation): "Stranger, report this word, we pray, to the Spartans, that lying here in this spot we remain, faithfully keeping their laws."
results.answer: According to Herodotus, the epitaph inscribed for the Spartans who fell at Thermopylae was: "Stranger, report this word, we pray, to the Spartans, that lying here in this spot we remain, faithfully keeping their laws" [2]. Grote renders a slightly different translation: "Stranger, tell the Lacedæmonians, that we lie here, in obedience to their orders" [1].

results.question: What was Augustus' boast about the city of Rome and what he did for it?
results.ideal_answer: Augustus so improved the city — which had not been built in a manner suited to the grandeur of the empire — that he boasted, in Suetonius' words, that he "found it of brick, but left it of marble."
results.answer: According to Suetonius, Augustus boasted that he 'found it of brick, but left it of marble' [2]. Suetonius explains that he made many improvements to the city, which had previously been unsuited to the empire's grandeur and prone to floods and fires. Augustus rendered it more secure against such disasters and erected numerous public buildings, including a new forum (built to handle the increased population and legal business) that contained the temple of Mars the Avenger, a temple of Apollo on the Palatine hill (with adjoining porticos and libraries), and the temple of Jupiter Tonans in the Capitol [2]. (There is also a variant recorded by Cassius Dio, noted in a footnote, where the boast is: 'That Rome, which I found built of mud, I shall leave you firm as a rock' [1].)
results.judge_notes: faithfulness: Every claim in the answer is directly supported by the source passages, including the boast, the city improvements, the specific buildings erected, and Dio's variant, with no substantive invented content. | completeness: The answer fully states Augustus' boast and comprehensively details what he did for Rome, including improvements, disaster prevention, and specific buildings erected. | attribution: The answer correctly surfaces the differing versions of Augustus' boast and explicitly attributes each version to its respective source (Suetonius and Cassius Dio) in prose.

results.question: What honor did Caligula plan to give his horse Incitatus?
results.ideal_answer: Suetonius reports it was said Caligula intended to make his horse Incitatus consul. He had already given the horse a marble stable, an ivory manger, purple housings, a jewelled frontlet, and a house with slaves and furniture for entertaining guests invited in the horse's name.

results.question: How does Caesar describe the division of Gaul at the start of his commentaries?
results.ideal_answer: Caesar opens by stating that all Gaul is divided into three parts, one inhabited by the Belgae, another by the Aquitani, and the third by the people called Celts in their own language and Gauls in Latin — all differing from each other in language, customs, and laws.
results.answer: At the start of his commentaries, Caesar states: "All Gaul is divided into three parts, one of which the Belgae inhabit, the Aquitani another, those who in their own language are called Celts, in ours Gauls, the third. All these differ from each other in language, customs and laws. The river Garonne separates the Gauls from the Aquitani; the Marne and the Seine separate them from the Belgae." [1]

results.question: What were Nero's last words about himself as he prepared to die?
results.ideal_answer: As his grave was prepared before his eyes, Nero wept and repeatedly said, "What an artist is now about to perish!"
results.answer: As Nero prepared to die, his last words about himself expressed regret at the loss of his artistic talent. Cassius Dio records a similar exclamation at the moment of his suicide: "Jupiter, what an artist perishes in me!" [3]. The Latin version of the phrase, "Qualis artifex pereo," is also noted by the modern historian Samuel Dill [2].

results.question: How many Jews perished and how many were taken captive during the whole siege of Jerusalem in the Jewish War?
results.ideal_answer: Josephus reports that the number carried captive during the whole war was ninety-seven thousand, and the number who perished during the whole siege was eleven hundred thousand (1,100,000) — most of them Jews who had come up from the country to Jerusalem for the feast of unleavened bread and were then shut in by the Roman army, destroyed by pestilence and famine.
results.answer: During the whole siege of Jerusalem, according to Josephus, the number of those that perished was 1,100,000 (one million one hundred thousand), and the number of those carried captive during the whole war was 97,000 (ninety-seven thousand) [1]. A footnote in the same work reiterates these figures of 1,100,000 dead and 97,000 captives [2]. Josephus notes that the vast majority of those who perished were not citizens of Jerusalem but Jews from all over the country who had come up for the feast of unleavened bread and were suddenly trapped by the Roman army [1].

results.question: What did Eleazar urge the defenders of Masada to do rather than fall into Roman hands?
results.ideal_answer: Eleazar urged the defenders to die free by their own hands rather than be taken — to slay their own wives and children and then one another, having first burned their possessions and the fortress, so as to preserve their freedom and deny the Romans the pleasure of capturing them. They carried out the mass suicide, sparing only their stores of food.

He framed this mass suicide as a noble act that would preserve their liberty and serve as an example to the Romans. [1][2]

results.question: What act of a starving mother during the famine in Jerusalem does Josephus describe in the Wars of the Jews?
results.ideal_answer: Josephus describes Mary of Bethezuba, a once-wealthy woman driven to madness by the famine, who killed her infant son, roasted him, ate one half and kept the other half concealed — an atrocity he says had no parallel in Greek or barbarian history.
results.answer: Josephus describes a woman named Mary, daughter of Eleazar from the village Bethezob, who during the famine in Jerusalem killed her infant son, roasted him, ate one half, and concealed the other half. When the seditious men entered her house and demanded food, she uncovered the remains of the child and told them to eat, saying she had already eaten half herself [3][1].

results.question: By what act could a man become the priest of Diana's grove at Nemi, and what was the priest called?
results.ideal_answer: A man could win the priesthood only by breaking off a bough of a certain sacred tree in the grove and then slaying the reigning priest in single combat; having killed him, he succeeded to the office and held it until he in turn was slain by a stronger or craftier challenger. The priest bore the title King of the Wood (Rex Nemorensis).
results.answer: A man became the priest of Diana's grove at Nemi by being a runaway slave who broke a branch from a sacred tree within the sanctuary, then fighting and slaying the incumbent priest in single combat. The priest was called the King of the Wood, or *Rex Nemorensis* [1].
results.judge_notes: faithfulness: Every claim in the answer is directly supported by the cited source passage [1]. | completeness: The answer fully covers both parts of the question: the acts required (breaking a branch from the sacred tree and slaying the incumbent priest in single combat) and the priest's title (King of the Wood / Rex Nemorensis), without omitting anything asked. | attribution: Only one source is used, and the answer correctly attributes the information to it without any misattribution.

results.question: What was the supreme or national god of the Assyrians?
results.ideal_answer: The supreme, distinctively national god of the Assyrians was Asshur — head of the Assyrian pantheon, styled "the great Lord" and "King of all the Gods," the tutelary deity of the kings and of the country, whose name was identical with that of Assyria itself.
results.answer: The supreme or national god of the Assyrians was Asshur (also spelled Asshur). He stood at the head of the Assyrian Pantheon as "the great god," bearing titles such as "the great Lord," "the King of all the Gods," and "he who rules supreme over the Gods" [1][2]. He was regarded as the especial tutelary deity of both the kings and the country, and his name was identical with that of the land itself [1].

results.question: What was the principal building material of Chaldaea, and why was stone so little used?
results.ideal_answer: The principal building material of Chaldaea was brick, made from the abundant clay of the alluvial plain (sun-dried, or kiln-baked for greater hardness). Stone was scarcely used because the alluvium was destitute of it — any stone had to be imported from adjacent countries — so the Chaldaeans built with clay brick in its default.
results.answer: The principal building material of Chaldaea was clay, which was moulded into bricks and hardened either by sun-drying or kiln-firing [1][4]. Stone was used very little because the alluvial plain of Chaldaea was entirely devoid of stone. Any stone required for building had to be imported from neighboring regions, such as the Arabian border, but the difficulty of transporting heavy stone across the soft, oozy soil of the plain prevented its adoption except to an extremely limited extent [1][4].

results.question: How many men were appointed to draw up the laws at Athens and became known as the Thirty?
results.ideal_answer: Thirty men were chosen — the body that became known as the Thirty (later the Thirty Tyrants) — appointed to draft a constitution based on the ancestral laws of Athens, though they never published the laws and instead seized power for themselves.
results.answer: Thirty men were appointed to draw up the laws at Athens. Xenophon records that "the people passed a resolution to choose thirty men who were to draft a constitution based on the ancestral laws of the State" and then lists all thirty by name [2]. George Grote likewise notes that a motion was carried to name "a Board of Thirty... to draw up laws for the future government of the city" [1].

results.question: What did Maharbal say to Hannibal after the battle of Cannae?
results.ideal_answer: When Hannibal hesitated to march at once on Rome after Cannae, his cavalry commander Maharbal — who had urged that within five days Hannibal might dine in the Capitol — rebuked him: "You know how to conquer, Hannibal, but you do not know how to make use of your victory." Livy adds that this day's delay is believed to have saved the city and the empire.
results.answer: After the battle of Cannae, when others were congratulating Hannibal and advising him to rest, Maharbal, the prefect of the cavalry, urged him to march immediately on Rome. He said: "Nay, rather, that you may know what has been achieved by this battle, five days hence you shall feast in triumph in the Capitol. Follow me: I will go first with the cavalry, that they may know that I am arrived before they know of me as approaching." Hannibal replied that he applauded Maharbal's zeal but needed time to ponder the proposal. Maharbal then retorted: "Of a truth the gods have not bestowed all things upon the same person. You know how to conquer, Hannibal; but you do not know how to make use of your victory." [1]
results.judge_notes: faithfulness: Every claim and direct quote in the answer is fully supported by the cited source passage [1], with no invented facts or unsupported attributions. | completeness: The answer fully captures what Maharbal said, including his urging to march on Rome, the prediction of dining in the Capitol within five days, and the famous rebuke that Hannibal knew how to conquer but not how to use his victory, thus covering everything the question asks for. | attribution: The answer correctly attributes the quotes and context to the single provided source without any misattribution or need for disagreement-surfacing.

results.question: What trophy of gold rings was poured out before the Carthaginian senate to show the scale of the victory at Cannae?
results.ideal_answer: To prove the scale of the victory, Mago had the gold rings stripped from the Roman knights slain at Cannae poured out in the vestibule of the Carthaginian senate-house — a heap that, by report, filled about a peck (some claimed three and a half pecks) — explaining that only knights, and of these the principal only, wore such rings.
results.answer: After the victory at Cannae, Hannibal's brother Mago poured out a heap of gold rings in the vestibule of the Carthaginian senate-house to demonstrate the scale of the Roman defeat. Livy reports that the rings were taken from Roman knights (the only class who wore such ornaments) and that the heap measured about a peck, though some sources claimed it was as much as three and a half pecks [1]. Cassius Dio similarly notes that Hannibal measured the rings by the quart and sent them to Carthage to indicate the number of fallen knights and senators [2].
