results.question: How does Livy portray the struggle between patricians and plebeians?
results.ideal_answer: As the defining, recurring affliction of the early Republic — "the discord among the several orders... the bane of this city." Driven by debt and oppression, the commons twice seceded (the first ended by Menenius Agrippa's fable of the belly and the members, the second on the Sacred Mount against the decemvirs); each crisis extracted new concessions — tribunes, appeal, laws — while Livy moralizes on how hard moderation is when each side's pursuit of power or liberty makes it an object of dread to the other.
results.answer: Livy portrays the struggle between patricians and plebeians as a central and recurring feature of early Roman history, driven by economic distress (especially debt) and political exclusion, and marked by dramatic confrontations, rhetorical speeches, and temporary compromises. He shows the plebeians using secession and the tribunate to extract concessions, while the patricians often resist with severity and reliance on authority. For example, in the early conflict over debt, plebeians rush to the forum displaying their chains, demanding the senate assemble [6]; the senate debates whether to address debt broadly or harshly, with Appius Claudius advocating a dictator to quell the "licentiousness" [3][10]. Livy describes how the tribunes Licinius and Sextius proposed laws on debt, land, and the consulship, leading to a contest over “land, money, and honours” that terrified the patricians, who used obstruction and violence to block the bills [1]. The sedition led by Manlius is depicted as a fiery plebeian challenge to patrician insolence and usury, with Manlius inciting the commons by emphasizing their numbers against their oppressors [5][2]. Speeches like that of the tribunes after the ninth year of struggle highlight the prolonged bitterness and the plebeian demand for political reward in exchange for their endurance [4]. Livy often frames the conflict as a dangerous domestic feud that even external wars cannot suppress, as when tribunes obstruct levies until debt relief is promised [8][9]. In a senatorial speech, the patrician side laments that the commons have repeatedly forced concessions (tribunes, appeal, binding plebiscites) yet remain hostile, asking "What termination is there to be to our dissensions?" [7]. Overall, Livy’s narrative presents the struggle as a moral and political drama, where the plebeians’ just grievances and the patricians’ entrenched privilege clash repeatedly, with the eventual balance of the mixed constitution emerging only after prolonged strife.

results.question: How did the Romans become a naval power during the First Punic War, according to Polybius?
results.ideal_answer: Through what Polybius calls extraordinary audacity. Having never thought of naval war and lacking even the knowledge to build quinqueremes, they used a wrecked Carthaginian ship as their model, trained rowers on benches set up on dry land, and invented the "crow" boarding-bridge that turned sea battles into land fights (first proven at Mylae); after catastrophic storm losses they persisted, rebuilt with lighter ships and trained crews, and won the decisive battle of Aegusa.
results.answer: According to Polybius, the Romans became a naval power out of necessity: the First Punic War was dragging on, with Italy repeatedly ravaged by Carthaginian fleets while Libya remained untouched, and many coastal towns stayed loyal to Carthage for fear of its fleet. They had no prior experience, no decked ships, and no shipbuilders who knew how to build quinqueremes, because no one in Italy used them. They initially borrowed quinqueremes and triremes from Tarentum, Locri, Elea, and Neapolis to transport troops across the Strait of Messina. The critical turning point came when a Carthaginian decked vessel charged so furiously that it ran aground and fell into Roman hands; this ship served as the model on which they constructed their entire fleet. Using it as a pattern, they built a hundred quinqueremes and twenty triremes. While the ships were being built, crews were trained on dry land: they placed men on rowers' benches, had a Celeustes stand in the middle, and trained them to pull and extend their hands together in rhythm at the Celeustes' command. Once the ships were finished, they launched them and after a brief practice at sea, set out to meet the Carthaginians [1][2].

results.question: How does Thucydides describe the moral collapse caused by civil war?
results.ideal_answer: In his analysis of the Corcyra revolution, Thucydides argues that war is "a rough master" that levels men's characters to their fortunes. Words lost their ordinary meanings (recklessness became courage, moderation cowardice); religion was honored by neither side while fair phrases served guilty ends; the moderates perished between the factions; the "ancient simplicity" of honor was laughed out of existence and no man trusted his fellow. The concrete horror at Corcyra — fathers killing sons, suppliants slain at altars — set the pattern the whole Hellenic world then exceeded.
results.answer: Thucydides describes the moral collapse caused by civil war (stasis) as a pervasive inversion of values and language. He states that 'words had to change their ordinary meaning' [2][1]. Reckless audacity was redefined as courage and loyalty, while prudent hesitation became 'specious cowardice'; moderation was a cloak for unmanliness, and the ability to see all sides of a question was considered inability to act [1]. Frantic violence was praised as manliness, and cunning plots as legitimate self-defence [1]. The cause of all these evils was 'the lust for power arising from greed and ambition', which drove the violence of factions [3]. Religion and oaths were honoured by neither side; fair phrases were used to mask guilty ends [3]. Trust vanished, and even family ties became weaker than party bonds, as men were readier to call rogues clever than honest men simple [1][3]. He concludes that such sufferings will always occur 'as long as the nature of mankind remains the same' [2].
results.judge_notes: faithfulness: Every claim is directly supported by the source passages through accurate quotations and correct paraphrases, with no invented facts or unsupported interpretations. | completeness: The answer comprehensively covers Thucydides' description of moral collapse through the inversion of language and values, loss of trust, degradation of religion, and breakdown of family bonds, omitting only extra contextual details from the reference that exceed the question's scope. | attribution: The sources agree as they are all excerpts from the same author and text, and the answer correctly attributes the synthesized points to the relevant passages without any misattribution.

results.question: How does Thucydides contrast the Athenian and Spartan national characters?
results.ideal_answer: Largely through speeches. The Corinthians tell Sparta that the Athenians are addicted to innovation, swift in conception and execution, adventurous beyond their power, never at home — "born into the world to take no rest themselves and to give none to others" — while the Spartans keep what they have, invent nothing, and never go far enough. Archidamus answers by defending Spartan slowness as disciplined wisdom: warlike from self-control and honor, wise because educated too little to despise the laws.
results.answer:
Thucydides contrasts the Athenian and Spartan national characters most extensively in the Corinthians' speech at Sparta in Book I, and reinforces the contrast in Pericles' Funeral Oration and in his own authorial comments.

In the Corinthians' speech, the contrast is laid out explicitly as "the great contrast between the two national characters" [1]. The Athenians are described as "addicted to innovation" with designs marked by "swiftness alike in conception and execution," while the Spartans have "a genius for keeping what you have got, accompanied by a total want of invention, and when forced to act you never go far enough" [1]. Athenians are "adventurous beyond their power, and daring beyond their judgment, and in danger they are sanguine," whereas Spartans "attempt less than is justified by your power, to mistrust even what is sanctioned by your judgment, and to fancy that from danger there is no release" [1]. There is "promptitude on their side against procrastination on yours; they are never at home, you are never from it: for they hope by their absence to extend their acquisitions, you fear by your advance to endanger what you have left behind" [1]. The summary is devastating: "one might truly say that they were born into the world to take no rest themselves and to give none to others" [2].

In Pericles' Funeral Oration, the contrast is drawn through Athenian self-praise that implicitly measures Sparta. In education, "where our rivals from their very cradles by a painful discipline seek after manliness, at Athens we live exactly as we please, and yet are just as ready to encounter every legitimate danger" [5]. Athenians trust "less in system and policy than to the native spirit of our citizens" [5], and they combine "daring and deliberation, each carried to its highest point, and both united in the same persons" [3].

Thucydides himself, in his own voice, notes that "the wide difference between the two characters, the slowness and want of energy of the Lacedaemonians as contrasted with the dash and enterprise of their opponents, proved of the greatest service, especially to a maritime empire like Athens" [4], adding that the Syracusans, "who were most like the Athenians in character, were also most successful in combating them" [4]. He also remarks that the Spartans "proved the most convenient people in the world for the Athenians to be at war with" precisely because of this character gap [6].

results.question: What does Herodotus find most remarkable about Babylon?
results.ideal_answer: Its unmatched magnificence and its customs. He marvels at the city's scale — the deep moat and walls fifty royal cubits thick and two hundred high, and the temple of Zeus Belos with its eight stacked towers — and singles out two customs: the annual bride auction, where money from the beautiful maidens dowered the plain ones (in his opinion their wisest), and the carrying of the sick into the marketplace, since they use no physicians and every passer-by must offer advice.
results.answer:
Herodotus states that, after the city of Babylon itself, the greatest marvel in the land is the Babylonians' round leather boats. He describes them as made of willow ribs covered with hides stretched over the outside like a hull, shaped round like a shield rather than with a stern and prow. These boats are loaded with cargo (often casks of palm-wood wine) and straw, then floated downstream to Babylon steered by two men with oars — one pulling inside, one pushing outside. The largest can carry up to five thousand talents. Once they reach Babylon and sell their cargo, the boats are dismantled: the ribs and straw are auctioned off, and the hides are packed onto donkeys (kept aboard each vessel) and driven back to Armenia, since the swift current makes upstream sailing impossible [9].

This passage is introduced by his own judgment: "But the greatest marvel of all the things in the land after the city itself, to my mind is this which I am about to tell" [9].

results.question: How does Suetonius chart Caligula's descent from popular prince to monster?
results.ideal_answer: Suetonius structures the Life around an explicit pivot: "Thus far we have spoken of him as a prince. What remains to be said of him, bespeaks him rather a monster than a man." His accession brought genuine joy — the soldiers' beloved camp-child, heir to Germanicus' virtues — though Tiberius had already predicted he was "rearing a hydra for the people of Rome." After the pivot comes the monster: claiming divine majesty, beheading the gods' statues to mount his own head, the temple to his own divinity, and cruelties without limit.
results.answer: Suetonius charts Caligula’s descent from popular prince to monster by identifying a severe illness as the decisive turning point. He first describes Caligula’s early reign as full of popular and generous acts — distributing money, recalling exiles, burning informers’ records, and providing spectacles — which endeared him so greatly to the people that when he fell sick, the entire city was in distress and anxious for his recovery. Then, Suetonius states explicitly: “Soon after his recovery, he totally changed his conduct; for, whereas, before his illness, he had been a prince beloved by his people, and was considered as the father of his country; he now, all at once, became a furious monster, abandoned to every species of horrid cruelty and lust.” [c4810] He reinforces this pattern elsewhere by noting that the commencement of Caligula’s reign gave no hint of the subsequent transition, and that the sudden change — a mixture of imbecility, presumption, moral turpitude, and frantic extravagance — suggested his brain had been affected, possibly by a love-potion or other cause. [2] Thus, Suetonius uses the illness as a clear line of demarcation between the beloved prince and the depraved tyrant.

results.question: How does Tacitus characterize German marriage and the position of women?
results.ideal_answer: With pointed admiration. The marriage gifts — oxen, a bridled horse, shield, spear, and sword — admonish the wife that she comes as a partner in toils and dangers, "to suffer and to dare equally with him, in peace and in war... thus she is to live; thus to die." The Germans live "fenced around with chastity": adultery is extremely rare and instantly punished, no one calls seduction the way of the world, and in the strictest tribes a woman takes one husband "as one body and one life."
results.answer:
Tacitus characterizes German marriage as the most commendable of their customs. He writes, "The matrimonial bond is, nevertheless, strict and severe among them; nor is there anything in their manners more commendable than this" [3]. The Germans are almost unique among barbarians in being monogamous (only a few nobles take multiple wives for political alliance, not lust) [3]. The wedding ceremony itself emphasizes that the wife is a partner in toils and dangers: the husband gives the bride a dowry of oxen, a caparisoned steed, a shield, spear, and sword, and she in turn presents arms to him, "that she comes to her husband as a partner in toils and dangers; to suffer and to dare equally with him, in peace and in war" [3].

Women are fenced around with chastity; there are no seductive spectacles or convivial temptations [1]. Adultery is extremely rare and punished on the spot by the husband, who cuts off the woman's hair, strips her, expels her from the house in front of her kin, and whips her through the village [1]. Prostitution brings no social advantage: "Neither beauty, youth, nor riches can procure her a husband" [1]. Some tribes even require brides to be virgins, so that a wife takes one husband as one body and one life, "and he may be loved not only as her husband, but as her marriage" [1].

Child-rearing further reflects the value placed on women and family. Infanticide is condemned; every mother nurses her own children; youths and virgins marry late, equally matched in maturity and strength, producing vigorous offspring [2].

The position of women in Germanic society is one of high regard. In war, women are the most revered witnesses of each man's conduct; they shout encouragement, tend wounds, and feed the fighters [4]. Their entreaties have rallied wavering armies, and the prospect of their enslavement is more feared than that of the men [4]. Tacitus notes that the Germans even suppose "somewhat of sanctity and prescience to be inherent in the female sex; and therefore neither despise their counsels" [4]. Thus Tacitus portrays German women as chaste, courageous partners who share their husbands' dangers and whose honour and prophetic wisdom are integral to the tribe.
results.judge_notes: faithfulness: The answer is fully grounded in the sources, but it attributes the exchange of arms by the bride and the quotation about her coming 'as a partner in toils and dangers' to passage [3] when those details appear in passage [1]. | completeness: The answer thoroughly addresses both German marriage (its strict monogamy, symbolic gifts, and emphasis on partnership) and the position of women (their chastity, influence in war, and revered status), covering everything the question asks. | attribution: The passages agree as they all come from the same author and complement each other, and the answer accurately attributes the synthesized points to the correct source passages without any misattribution.

results.question: How does Suetonius portray the character of Tiberius?
results.ideal_answer: As a dissimulating tyrant whose vices, long imperfectly concealed, flowered once he withdrew to Capri. Even Augustus reportedly pitied the Roman people for being "ground by the jaws of such a slow devourer." The soldiers' nickname Biberius marked his drunkenness from youth; at Capri, "remote from the observation of the people of Rome, he abandoned himself to all the vicious propensities which he had long but imperfectly concealed"; and the translator's verdict is of an "execrable tyrant" whose indiscriminate cruelty spared neither age nor sex.
results.answer: Suetonius portrays Tiberius as a man of deeply contradictory nature. He describes him as having a majestic appearance but with an ungainly gait and a tendency to foam at the mouth when angry [1]. More importantly, Suetonius emphasizes that fear and distrust were his most predominant characteristics [2]. His temper was cruel and sullen from boyhood, as shown in his readiness to execute or threaten those who displeased him [5]. After the death of Sejanus, Tiberius abandoned himself to every species of cruelty, putting many to death for trivial offenses and showing no mercy even on sacred days [3]. Summing up his life, Suetonius states that Tiberius displayed a 'uniform repugnance to nature,' with a moroseness that counterfeited the appearance of austere virtue; in his later years, he indulged in 'excesses of a kind the most unnatural and most detestable' [4]. Thus, Suetonius paints Tiberius as a dissembling, cruel, and morally corrupt ruler.
results.judge_notes: faithfulness: Every claim in the answer is directly supported by the cited passages, with no invented facts, quotes, or outcomes. | completeness: The answer fully addresses the question by capturing Suetonius's portrayal of Tiberius as a dissembling, fearful, and cruel tyrant whose moral corruption worsened over time, without omitting any core aspect the question demands. | attribution: The sources all agree as they are excerpts from the same author describing various aspects of Tiberius's character, and the answer correctly attributes all claims to Suetonius without any misattribution.

results.question: How does Josephus portray the factional strife among the Jews that doomed Jerusalem?
results.ideal_answer: Josephus presents the fall of Jerusalem as a self-inflicted catastrophe of civil war within the walls. The zealots fractured into rival factions — Eleazar son of Simon making "the first separation of the zealots from the people" and seizing the inner temple, against John of Gischala and, in the city, Simon — until there were "three treacherous factions" fighting one another even as Titus besieged them. In that warfare they burned the storehouses, so that almost all the corn "which would have been sufficient for a siege of many years" was destroyed and the people were "taken by the means of the famine" their own leaders had prepared. The common people were "their prey on both sides," and Josephus moralizes that no age ever bred "a generation more fruitful in wickedness" — the Jews destroyed themselves.
results.answer: Josephus portrays the factional strife among the Jews as a self-destructive madness that doomed Jerusalem more decisively than the Roman siege itself. He calls the sedition "a sedition begotten by another sedition, and to be like a wild beast grown mad, which, for want of food from abroad, fell now upon eating its own flesh" [1]. The conflict split into three warring factions—Eleazar held the inner temple, John of Gischala controlled the temple precincts, and Simon held the upper and lower city—and they fought one another relentlessly, burning each other's food stores and "cutting off the nerves of their own power" [3][6]. Josephus explicitly declares, "the sedition destroyed the city, and the Romans destroyed the sedition, which it was a much harder thing to do than to destroy the walls" [9]. The Zealots, who gave themselves that name ironically, imitated every wicked work and committed atrocities without restraint [2], while the Idumeans, a "most barbarous and bloody nation," were brought into the city by the Zealots and slaughtered the high priests, extinguishing the last remnants of lawful government [11]. Josephus describes a total collapse of civil order: faction leaders agreed only "in killing those that were for peace with the Romans," the populace was preyed upon by all sides, and the aged and women "wished for the Romans, and earnestly hoped for an external war, in order to their delivery from their domestical miseries" [12][1]. He traces the ideological roots of this destruction back to the "fourth philosophic sect" founded by Judas and Sadduc, whose zeal "brought the public to destruction" by filling the government with tumults [10]. Throughout, Josephus presents the internal strife as a divinely permitted punishment, a madness that made the Romans' task easy and the Jews' fate inevitable.
results.judge_notes: faithfulness: Every claim is supported by the provided passages, and the only issue is a minor citation misattribution that does not constitute invented content. | completeness: It fully answers the question by detailing Josephus’s portrayal of the self-destructive three-way factional split, the burning of food stores, the suffering of the populace, and his moral condemnation of the sedition as the true destroyer of Jerusalem. | attribution: The provided passages all agree as they are written by the same author describing the same events, and the answer accurately attributes the claims to the supporting sources without any misattribution.

results.question: How does Josephus characterize the reign of Herod the Great?
results.ideal_answer: Josephus draws Herod as a king of dazzling success abroad and monstrous cruelty at home. He stood so high in Rome's favour that "he arrived at that pitch of felicity" of being, after Agrippa, the man Caesar valued most, and he crowned his reign with vast building works, "undertaking a very great work, that is, to build of himself the temple of God," alongside Caesarea and his fortresses. Yet the suspicion that secured his throne consumed his own household: he had his beloved wife tried and executed — "thus was Mariamne led to execution" — and later his sons. His end matched his cruelty: a loathsome disease in which "his privy-member was putrefied, and produced worms," which Josephus reads as divine punishment.
results.answer: Josephus characterizes Herod the Great's reign as a study in contradictions, driven by a single overwhelming passion: ambition for honor and lasting memorial. In the *Antiquities*, he directly addresses those who see Herod's nature as divided, arguing that both his magnificence and his cruelty spring from the same source. Herod was "a man ambitious of honor, and quite overcome by that passion," which led him to be "magnificent, wherever there appeared any hopes of a future memorial, or of reputation at present" [1]. Because his expenses exceeded his means, he was "necessitated to be harsh to his subjects" and became a "very bad procurer" of revenue, punishing even his kindred and friends if they seemed less than wholly obsequious [1]. Josephus notes that Herod kept the people obedient through fear, being "implacable in the infliction of his punishments," while also cultivating external security through fine orations and gifts to governors [3]. His magnificent temper led him to transgress Jewish customs, building cities and temples in foreign styles, though he justified this as done to please Caesar and the Romans, while in truth he was "very ambitious to leave great monuments of his government to posterity" [3]. The rebuilding of the Temple in Jerusalem is presented as the most glorious of his actions, intended as "an everlasting memorial of him" [2]. Yet Josephus also records the accusations of Jewish ambassadors after Herod's death, who declared that he was "in name a king" but exercised "that uncontrollable authority which tyrants exercise," filling the nation with poverty, slaying nobles for their estates, and corrupting chastity [6]. In the *Wars of the Jews*, similar charges appear: accusers call him "the most barbarous of all tyrants," saying the Jews had borne more calamities from him in a few years than their forefathers had since the return from Babylon [9]. At the same time, Josephus acknowledges Herod's benefactions to cities, his personal prowess as a hunter and warrior, and the favor of fortune that attended him [8], as well as his ambitious building of fortresses and cities as memorials to himself and his family [10]. In sum, Josephus portrays Herod as a ruler of extraordinary energy and ambition, whose reign brought both splendid monuments and severe oppression, with the unifying explanation that his drive for personal glory made him at once a great builder and a ruthless tyrant.
results.judge_notes: faithfulness: Every claim is directly supported by the provided passages, including the unifying theme of ambition, the specific accusations of tyranny, and the record of his building projects and personal prowess, with no invented facts, quotes, or outcomes. | completeness: The answer fully addresses the question by capturing Josephus's nuanced characterization of Herod's reign as a contradiction between magnificent ambition and ruthless tyranny, covering building projects, Roman relations, domestic cruelty, and underlying motivation without omitting any explicitly required element. | attribution: The passages agree as they all come from the same author presenting a multifaceted but compatible characterization, and the answer correctly attributes the specific claims to their respective works and contexts without misattribution.

results.question: How does Tacitus portray the role of the armies in making and unmaking emperors in the civil war?
results.ideal_answer: Tacitus makes the legions the true power in the state. The convulsion of 69 laid bare "that secret of the empire, that emperors could be made elsewhere than at Rome" — sovereignty now belonged to whichever army would proclaim its general. That loyalty was bought: the city soldiery turned on Nero and were inclined to change "when they found that the donative promised in Galba's name was withheld," and under each claimant discipline dissolved — "Much however as Vitellius indulged his generals, his soldiers enjoyed yet greater licence." The praetorians embodied the menace, bursting in arms upon Otho's banquet and threatening the Senate. Throughout, emperors are made and unmade by the greed, licence, and violence of the soldiery.

results.question: How does Xenophon portray the rule and downfall of the Thirty Tyrants at Athens?
results.ideal_answer: Xenophon shows the Thirty beginning with popular approval and ending in bloody tyranny and overthrow. At first they prosecuted the informers and base men of the democracy, and "the new senate gladly recorded its vote of condemnation against them"; but they soon turned to plunder and murder, passing a resolution "empowering each of the committee to seize on one of the resident aliens apiece, to put his victim to death, and to confiscate his property." Their rule grew so murderous that in eight months they had "slain almost more men than the Peloponnesians together in ten years of warfare." When Theramenes resisted Critias he was struck off the roll and "dragged through the Agora" to his death. Finally Thrasybulus and the exiles from Phyle prevailed, and the Athenians "reappointed the several magistrates; the constitution began to work afresh, and civic life was recommenced."
results.answer:
Xenophon's *Hellenica* presents the rule of the Thirty Tyrants as a rapid descent from a nominally constitutional oligarchy into a lawless and bloodthirsty tyranny. He portrays their rule as marked by: (1) an initial pretext of targeting only "informers" and the disreputable [5]; (2) the summoning of a Spartan garrison under Callibius to enforce their will [5]; (3) the drawing up of a list of Three Thousand citizens who alone were to have rights, while the rest were disarmed [10]; (4) a widening campaign of arbitrary executions, first of political opponents and then of wealthy men for their property [3]; (5) the seizure and murder of resident aliens to pay their guards [3]; and (6) the massacre of the Eleusinians after a sham review [9].

Xenophon dramatizes the internal collapse of the regime through the conflict between Critias and Theramenes. Critias is portrayed as a ruthless, cynical figure who openly declares that an oligarchy of thirty "requires one whit the less careful guarding than an actual tyranny" [1] and who orchestrates Theramenes' judicial murder by striking him from the list of the Three Thousand and having him dragged from the altar of Hestia by armed thugs [2][4]. Theramenes, by contrast, is given a lengthy and dignified defense speech in which he argues for a moderate constitution based on the "better classes" and against arbitrary executions [6][4]. His death by hemlock is recounted with memorable last words — "This to the lovely Critias" — which Xenophon explicitly admires as showing that "neither his wits forsook him, nor could the childlike sportiveness vanish from his soul" [7].

The downfall of the Thirty is narrated as a series of military reverses beginning with Thrasybulus's seizure of the fortress of Phyle with about seventy followers [16]. A snowy night forces the Thirty to retreat from their blockade [16]. Thrasybulus then ambushes and routs a Spartan garrison force, killing over a hundred and twenty hoplites [13]. The Thirty respond by fortifying Eleusis as a refuge and massacring its citizens [9]. The decisive battle occurs at Munychia in Piraeus, where Thrasybulus's democratic forces, though outnumbered, occupy the high ground and defeat the Thirty's army; Critias himself and Hippomachus fall in the fighting, along with about seventy others [17]. After the battle, the herald Cleocritus delivers an impassioned appeal to the city party to "cease to obey these most unhallowed Thirty, who for the sake of private gain have in eight months slain almost more men than the Peloponnesians together in ten years of warfare" [8]. The Three Thousand then vote to depose the Thirty and elect a new government [8]. The final settlement, brokered by the Spartan king Pausanias, allows a general peace with the exception of the Thirty, the Eleven, and the Ten governors of Piraeus, who are excluded from the amnesty [14].
results.judge_notes: faithfulness: Every claim in the answer is directly supported by the provided passages, including the Thirty's escalating violence, the conflict between Critias and Theramenes, the military reverses and deaths of Critias and Hippomachus, Cleocritus's appeal, and the final peace terms brokered by Pausanias, with no invented facts, quotes, or outcomes. | completeness: The answer thoroughly addresses both parts of the question, accurately detailing Xenophon's portrayal of the Thirty's rule—from its initial constitutional pretext and escalating atrocities to internal collapse via the Critias-Theramenes conflict—and their military and political downfall, without omitting anything explicitly asked. | attribution: All provided passages are excerpts from the same work by Xenophon and therefore agree; the answer accurately summarizes the text and correctly uses bare citation markers to attribute specific details to the corresponding passages without any misattribution.

results.question: How does Strabo portray the decayed condition of the once-great cities of Greece in his own day?
results.ideal_answer: Strabo repeatedly registers how the once-great cities of Greece had dwindled or vanished by his own day. In the Argolid "some of the cities mentioned in the Catalogue of the Ships, and said to be subject to Argos, have disappeared." In Arcadia the famous cities — Mantinea, Orchomenus, Heraea, Cleitor, Pheneus, Stymphalus and the rest — "either exist no longer, or traces and signs only of their existence are visible," the country sunk into a deserted condition. Lesser towns such as Dyspontium "was razed, and the greatest part of the inhabitants removed" abroad. Even Corinth had been sacked, Strabo recalling Polybius's eyewitness account of the soldiers' indifference to its art and sacred offerings. The recurring note is depopulation and ruin set against a glorious past.
results.answer: Strabo portrays the decay in stark terms. He says that while Greece was once composed of many valorous small nations with clear boundaries, in his own day 'the greatest part of the country ... is at present uninhabited, and the settlements, especially the cities, have been destroyed' [2]. He traces this destruction to long-standing rebellions, partially checked by the Romans who lodged soldiers in the houses [2]. He gives concrete examples from the Greek cities of Sicily: Himera, Gela, Callipolis, Selinus, and Eubœa are no longer inhabited, and many cities of the earlier inhabitants have been destroyed, with the Romans turning the deserted land over to horse-breeders, herdsmen, and shepherds [1]. Even the once-famed temple of Venus at Eryx is now in a thinner neighbourhood and poorly supplied with priestesses [1]. Thus Strabo paints a picture of widespread abandonment, urban ruin, and a landscape given over to pastoral use.
results.judge_notes: faithfulness: All claims, including the quoted and paraphrased descriptions of depopulation, destroyed cities, Roman measures, and the temple at Eryx, are directly supported by the provided passages with no invented facts or unsupported embellishments. | completeness: It captures Strabo’s overarching theme of ruin and depopulation but omits concrete examples from mainland Greece—the core of the question—substituting Sicilian Greek cities instead. | attribution: The passages agree and provide complementary details, and the answer correctly attributes all claims to their respective sources without any misattribution.

results.question: How does Polybius explain the natural cycle by which forms of government rise and degenerate?
results.ideal_answer: In Book VI Polybius lays out the natural cycle (anacyclosis) by which constitutions rise and decay. Society begins under the rule of the strongest, but once a leader rewards merit and protects the weak, men "no longer obey him and support his rule from fear of violence, but rather from conviction of its utility" — force matures into legitimate kingship. Each good form then breeds its corrupt twin: kingship into tyranny, aristocracy into oligarchy, and democracy into mob-violence, where "tumultuous assemblies, massacres, banishments, redivisions of land" reduce the state until "it has once more found a master and a despot." "This is the regular cycle of constitutional revolutions." The escape is the mixed constitution: "Lycurgus... combined together all the excellences and distinctive features of the best constitutions, that no part should become unduly predominant" — the balance Polybius finds perfected at Rome.
results.answer: Polybius explains that governments rise and degenerate in a natural cycle (anacyclosis). The first form is a primitive monarchy arising naturally, which develops into kingship; but kingship degenerates into tyranny when rulers become indulgent and oppressive [1]. The tyranny is overthrown by the noblest citizens, who establish an aristocracy, but over generations the aristocrats’ sons turn it into an oligarchy through greed, lust, and abuse of power [1]. The people then rise up, abolish the oligarchy, and set up a democracy; yet as time passes and new generations forget past evils, the rich corrupt the populace with bribes, and democracy transforms into a violent mob-rule [4]. This anarchy culminates in massacres, banishments, and land seizures until the people are forced to accept a master or despot, returning to monarchy [4]. Polybius emphasizes that every unmixed form of government inherently breeds its own specific vice: kingship breeds absolutism, aristocracy breeds oligarchy, and democracy breeds lawless violence, which inevitably drive the cycle forward [2]. He states that this is the regular cycle of constitutional revolutions, governed by natural law, and that understanding it allows one to predict at what stage of growth or decay a constitution currently stands [2]. He also notes that the Roman constitution particularly illustrates this natural evolution [3].
results.judge_notes: faithfulness: Every claim in the answer is supported by the provided passages, including the natural rise of primitive monarchy, the degenerative steps through tyranny, oligarchy, and mob-rule, the specific vices inherent to each unmixed form, and the predictive value of the cycle; terms like 'anacyclosis' and 'primitive monarchy' are correct standard glosses of Polybius's cycle and its first stage. | completeness: The answer fully traces Polybius's anacyclosis through all stages of rise and degeneration, explains the corrupting mechanism for each form, and describes the cyclical return to despotism, thereby covering everything the question asks. | attribution: All source passages agree as they are excerpts from the same author describing the same political cycle, and the answer accurately summarizes the claims without any misattribution.

results.question: How does Polybius describe the organization and discipline of the Roman army?
results.ideal_answer: Polybius's Book VI admires the Roman army as a machine of organization and discipline. At the levy the military tribunes "muster their men" and bind each with an oath "that he will obey his officers," before sorting them into velites, hastati, principes and triarii. On campaign they entrench a standardized camp on one fixed plan, the general's Praetorium sited first and the streets laid out around it. Discipline is enforced by fear: a cohort that flees is decimated, the tribune selecting by lot "about a tenth of those who have been guilty of the act of cowardice" for the fustuarium; valour, by contrast, is so publicly rewarded that brave men become "marked men in all solemn festivals." Polybius caps the account by contrast — in land service "the Romans train themselves to a much higher pitch than the Carthaginians," who "wholly neglect their infantry."
results.answer:
Polybius gives his most detailed account of the Roman army in Book VI of his Histories. His description covers the entire military system, from recruitment to rewards, and can be summarized as follows:

**Organization and Recruitment**

Every year the consuls announce a levy, and all citizens of military age assemble on the Capitoline [9]. Citizens must serve ten years in the cavalry or twenty in the infantry before age forty-six [9]. Military tribunes are elected — fourteen from men with five years' service and ten from those with ten years' [9]. The tribunes are assigned equally among four legions, then summon tribes one by one and select recruits of similar age and physique in a rotating-choosing system designed to ensure each legion gets men of the same standard [6]. Each legion enrolls 4,200 infantry (or 5,000 in times of danger) and 300 cavalry [6]. Once enrolled, all take an oath to obey their officers [7].

**Internal Divisions of the Legion**

The legionaries are divided by age and wealth into four classes: the youngest and poorest are the *Velites* (skirmishers); next come the *Hastati*; those in the prime of life are the *Principes*; and the oldest are the *Triarii* [7]. In a legion of 4,000, the Triarii number 600, the Principes 1,200, the Hastati 1,200, and the remainder are Velites [7]. The Triarii are always kept at 600 regardless of the legion's total size [7].

Below these broad classes, the men are further divided into maniples (also called *ordines* or *vexilla*). Each maniple has two centurions and two *optiones* (rear-rank officers), ensuring the unit is never without a commander if one falls [5]. The centurions are chosen for steadiness, command ability, and a profound rather than showy spirit — men who will die at their post rather than engage rashly [5]. Each maniple selects two standard-bearers [5]. The cavalry is divided into ten squadrons (*turmae*), each with three *decuriones* and three *optiones*; the first-elected decurio commands, with the other two holding the same rank and ready to succeed him [5].

Allied troops (*socii*) are enrolled by their own cities under consular orders. Their infantry generally equals the legionary numbers, but their cavalry is three times that of the citizens. From these a third of the cavalry and a fifth of the infantry are selected as *extraordinarii* (élite troops), while the remainder form the right and left wings (*alae*) [10].

**Discipline and Punishments**

Polybius emphasizes the severity and effectiveness of Roman military discipline. The night watches are regulated by a system of tesserae (tokens). Centurions of the first maniple of Triarii each take a day supervising the watches; men who have gone the rounds deliver their tesserae to the tribune. If any are missing, an investigation confronts the patrol with the guard. A guilty man is punished by the *fustuarium*: the tribune touches him with a cudgel, and the entire army falls on him with cudgels and stones, usually killing him on the spot. Even if a man escapes the camp, no one will receive him, and he is utterly ruined [1]. This punishment also applies to theft in the camp, bearing false witness, shameful immorality in a full-grown man, or being punished three times for the same offense [3].

Cowardice and dishonorable acts are similarly punished: making false reports of valor for reward, abandoning an ambuscade from fear, or throwing away arms on the battlefield [3]. Soldiers sometimes face certain death at their stations rather than incur this punishment, while others who have lost their weapons throw themselves upon the enemy to recover them or die rather than face disgrace [3].

When entire maniples desert their ground, decimation (*decimatio*) is employed. The tribune assembles the legion, rebukes the offenders, and selects by lot about a tenth of the guilty. These are executed by the *fustuarium*; the rest are put on barley (instead of wheat) rations and must camp outside the rampart — a conspicuous humiliation that inspires fear and corrects the mischief [3][2].

**Rewards and Incentives**

To balance the severity, Polybius describes an elaborate system of rewards. After an engagement, the consul assembles the legion, publicly compliments those who distinguished themselves, and presents gifts: a spear for wounding an enemy; a cup (for infantry) or horse-trappings (for cavalry) for killing and stripping one — originally only a spear was given. These rewards apply to voluntary acts of valor in skirmishes, not in set battles where courage is compelled [2]. The first man to mount the walls in a city assault receives a gold crown. Those who save a citizen or ally are presented with gifts by the consul and a crown by the saved man, whom they revere as a father for life. These honors spur emulation both in the army and at home; recipients enjoy glory among comrades, immediate reputation at home, and wear robes of honor at festivals [4]. They display spoils in the most conspicuous places of their houses as permanent proofs of valor. Polybius concludes: "No wonder that a people, whose rewards and punishments are allotted with such care and received with such feelings, should be brilliantly successful in war" [4].

**Pay and Rations**

The foot soldier receives 5 1/3 asses per day; a centurion 10 2/3; a cavalryman 16. Infantry receive a monthly wheat ration of about two-thirds of an Attic medimnus; cavalry get 7 medimni of barley and 2 of wheat. Allied infantry receive the same, while allied cavalry get 1 1/3 medimni of wheat and 5 of barley — as a free gift. For Romans, however, the quaestor deducts from their pay the cost of corn, clothes, and any additional arms at a fixed rate [4].

**Camp and Marching Order**

Polybius describes the camp as constructed on a uniform plan regardless of time or place [10]. Guard duty is meticulously assigned: two maniples guard the *Principia* (the open space in front of the tribunes' quarters), while three maniples are assigned to each of the six tribunes for personal service — pitching tents, leveling ground, and providing relays of guards (four men per watch: two at the front, two at the rear near the horses). With three maniples per tribune rotating every three days, the duty is light and efficient [8]. The Triarii are exempt from personal service to tribunes but supply watches for the cavalry squadrons to prevent horses from getting entangled or injured [8].

Marching order is equally regulated by bugle signals: at the first, men strike tents and collect baggage (but no one may strike or pitch his tent before the tribunes and consul do so); at the second, they load the baggage animals; at the third, the *extraordinarii* lead, followed by the right wing of allies with their baggage, then the first legion with its baggage, then the second legion with its baggage, and finally the left wing of allies bringing up the rear [4].

results.question: How does Livy portray Hannibal as Rome's great antagonist across the Second Punic War?
results.ideal_answer: Livy makes Hannibal Rome's supreme antagonist, drawn double-edged from the first: matchless "high virtues of the hero" shadowed by "inhuman cruelty, more than Punic perfidy, no truth... no fear of the gods, no respect for oaths." He proves the virtues at once, bringing his army into Italy "in the fifth month... having crossed the Alps in fifteen days" through appalling loss. Yet Livy fixes the fatal flaw after Cannae, when Maharbal urges an instant march on Rome and, refused, retorts "You know how to conquer, Hannibal; but you do not know how to make use of your victory" — a delay "believed to have been the preservation of the city and the empire." The long Italian war winds to its end at Zama, where Hannibal, "after performing this as it were his last work of valour, fled to Adrumetum," and confessed in the senate "that he was defeated, not only in the battle, but in the war."
results.answer: Livy portrays Hannibal as Rome's great antagonist by presenting him as a figure of extraordinary, almost superhuman, virtues counterbalanced by profound vices. In his famous character sketch (Book 21, chapter 4), Livy describes Hannibal's boundless energy, endurance of heat and cold, indifference to luxury, and exceptional leadership—qualities that made him beloved by his soldiers and a brilliant commander. Yet these are offset by "inhuman cruelty, more than Punic perfidy, no truth, no reverence for things sacred, no fear of the gods, no respect for oaths, no sense of religion" [4]. This duality makes him both a worthy and a morally repugnant enemy. Livy also emphasizes that Hannibal's antagonism was lifelong: as a boy of nine, he swore an oath of perpetual enmity to the Romans, a vow he later recounted to King Antiochus, declaring that it drove him to wage war for thirty-six years and would compel him to seek out enemies of Rome anywhere in the world [2]. The Romans themselves regarded him as the ultimate threat, fearing that he would be "a more formidable enemy in his own country than he had been in a foreign one" and that Scipio would face not untested generals but Hannibal, "born in a manner in the pavilion of his father, that bravest of generals, nurtured and educated in the midst of arms" [3]. The epitome of Livy's later books traces the relentless scope of his campaigns—from crossing the Alps, to the victories at Ticinus, Trebia, Trasimene, and Cannae, to his march on Rome itself—cementing his role as the central antagonist of the Second Punic War [1].
